coredata.dc:description: In this study, the polyvinylpyrrolidone-alizarin nanoparticles (PVP-AZ NPs) with favorable water dispersion and the carbon quantum dots (RQDs) with aggregate induced emission effect were synthesized to construct an eco-friendly film for food freshness monitoring. The introduction of PVP-AZ NPs and RQDs enhanced the network structure and thermal stability of the cassava starch/polyvinyl alcohol film, and reduced its crystallinity and light transmittance via non-covalent binding with the film-forming matrix. The developed film exhibited visually recognizable colorimetric and fluorescent responses to ammonia at 0.025–25 mg/mL, and it can be reused at least 6 times. Practical application experiment proved that the film, as an indicator label, can achieve accurate, real-time, and visual dynamic monitoring of the freshness of shrimp stored at 25 °C, 4 °C, and − 20 °C under daylight (orange yellow to purple) and UV light (red to blue). The integration of multivariate detection technology can eliminate the interference of external factors by self-correction to improve sensitivity and reliability, which provides a reference for the development of other food quality and safety monitoring platforms.

head.ce:title: A novel dual-channel cassava starch/polyvinyl alcohol-based film for visual monitoring of shrimp freshness

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, the polyvinylpyrrolidone-alizarin nanoparticles (PVP-AZ NPs) with favorable water dispersion and the carbon quantum dots (RQDs) with aggregate induced emission effect were synthesized to construct an eco-friendly film for food freshness monitoring. The introduction of PVP-AZ NPs and RQDs enhanced the network structure and thermal stability of the cassava starch/polyvinyl alcohol film, and reduced its crystallinity and light transmittance via non-covalent binding with the film-forming matrix. The developed film exhibited visually recognizable colorimetric and fluorescent responses to ammonia at 0.025–25 mg/mL, and it can be reused at least 6 times. Practical application experiment proved that the film, as an indicator label, can achieve accurate, real-time, and visual dynamic monitoring of the freshness of shrimp stored at 25 °C, 4 °C, and − 20 °C under daylight (orange yellow to purple) and UV light (red to blue). The integration of multivariate detection technology can eliminate the interference of external factors by self-correction to improve sensitivity and reliability, which provides a reference for the development of other food quality and safety monitoring platforms.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: The global circulation of high-protein foods is becoming increasingly common. However, improper transportation and storage conditions may lead to the proliferation of microorganisms that break down amino acids in proteins to produce bioamines (BAs) through decarboxylation (Kim et al., 2022; Miao, Wu, Li, & Zhang, 2023). The accumulation of these BAs can serve as a direct indicator of food spoilage (Liu, Xiao, Shang, Zhuang, & Wang, 2022). Therefore, it is urgent to develop a convenient, rapid, and accurate detection method to monitor BAs content for assessing the freshness of protein-rich foods.

In recent years, synthetic dyes such as methyl red, bromothymol blue, and bromocresol green had been developed as intelligent response labels embedded into biomacromolecule matrices as colorimetric signals (Komali, Gaikwad, & Yadav, 2022). When food spoilage produces BAs, the resulting change in pH alters the structure of these pigments, causing visually recognizable color changes (Xu et al., 2023). However, these chemically sensitive compounds were not edible and direct contact with food products increased the risk to consumers. They were potentially toxic and mutagenic during food storage and might be released from packaging materials. This limited their use in assessing food quality and safety. Therefore, current research focused on extracting natural pigments from plants (anthocyanins, betaine, and curcumin) to solve these problems (Cheng et al., 2023; Zhao et al., 2022). However, poor stability limits their practical application in functional labels (Guo, Lv, Hu, Du, & Lv, 2021; Li et al., 2023). In contrast, alizarin (AZ) exhibits excellent stability, sensitive color response to different pH, low cost, and green environmental protection (Ezati, Rhim, Moradi, Tajik, & Molaei, 2020; Wang et al., 2023). Based on this, Li, Lei, Song, Shuang, and Dong (2021) achieved colorimetric detection of hydrogen peroxide and peroxynitrite using AZ as an indicator. Wang et al. (2022) developed a gelatin matrix based active AZ-rich smart packaging film for monitoring the quality of fresh shrimp. Nonetheless, the low water solubility makes it easy to accumulate in the label, which affects the uniformity of the film color and the accuracy of the detection.

In addition, most reported smart labels are based on single response signal and face challenges including weak anti-interference ability and unstable detection results (Zhao et al., 2022). A dual-mode labeling system can provide different signals for self-correction, resulting in more reliable information (Li et al., 2023). Fluorescence analysis is another low-cost, fast, and highly sensitive technique (Liu et al., 2022). To date, various novel fluorescent materials including metal-organic frameworks, gold nanoparticles, pyridine salts, polyheterocycles and others have been widely used to construct fluorescent labels for BAs detection (Andre et al., 2022; Basavaraja et al., 2020; Xu, Lian, Hao, Zhang, & Yan, 2017). However, the detection sensitivity of these fluorescent materials in solid phase matrix is easily reduced due to the aggregation quenching effect at high concentration (Miao et al., 2023). The proposed of aggregation-induced emission (AIE) effect brings hope to solve this problem. Fluorescent materials with AIE characteristics can emit strong fluorescent signals at high concentrations or in solid states, thereby improving the signal-to-noise ratio and optical stability. Liu, Jiang, et al. (2022) constructed an AIE-rich smart fluorescent tag for non-contact and visual monitoring of seafood freshness. However, to our knowledge, there have been very limited reports on the use of fluorophore with AIE effect as a functional filler sensitive to BAs in the development of biomolecular smart packaging materials.

Cassava starch, as a natural and renewable biopolymer, possesses remarkable attributes such as low cost, elevated amylopectin content, substantial viscosity, safety assurance, edibility, and biodegradability (Qin, Liu, Zhang, & Liu, 2020). In general, cassava starch film, as the substrate for polymer films, exhibits excellent barrier properties (Lin, Peng, Chen, Li, & Cui, 2023). Estevez-Areco, Guz, Candal, and Goyanes (2020) used a cassava starch active double-layer film containing ZnO nanorods and a polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) electrostatic spinning pad containing rosemary extract to inhibit the growth of E. coli. Consequently, it exhibits immense potential for application in the realm of food packaging. However, when pure cassava starch is used as a food packaging material, its poor mechanical properties and strong hydrophilicity limit its application in food packaging. Therefore, this study designed an effective way to improve the physical properties of cassava starch by mixing it with other polymers.

The current study proposed a hypothesis that the incorporation of AZ nanoparticles with superior water dispersion and a fluorophore exhibiting AIE effect into cassava starch/PVA substrates would construct a colorimetric/fluorescent dual-channel indicator label capable of facilitating real-time visual monitoring of the freshness status of high-protein foods. We synthesized excellent water-soluble polypyrrolidone-AZ nanoparticles (PVP-AZ NPs) by coupling reaction of AZ with food additive (PVP), and used them as colorimetric indicators. Carbon quantum dots (RQDs) with AIE effect were synthesized via solvothermal reaction, which served as fluorescent indicators. It has excellent properties such as chemical stability, photostability, biocompatibility, and simple sample preparation. Furthermore, a novel colorimetric/fluorescent dual-mode smart label was developed by adding PVP-AZ NPs and RQDs to cassava starch/PVA matrix (Fig. 1 ). The as-prepared film was named the PVP-AZ NPs/RQDs (PA-R film). To the best of our knowledge, studies on the formation of colorimetric fluorescent dual-channel labels for visual monitoring of shrimp freshness by AZ-coupled PVP to form water-dispersible PVP-AZ NPs as pH-responsive signals and RQDs with AIE effects as fluorescence-responsive signals co-embedded in cassava starch/PVA matrix were limited. When BAs was produced from food spoilage, the phenol hydroxyl group of PVP-AZ NPs in the PA-R film undergoes a deprotonation reaction, resulting in the film color to change from orange yellow to purple under daylight. The RQDs in the film changes from the aggregation state to the dispersion state, causing the film to vary from red fluorescence to blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light. Besides, the blue fluorescence is weakened due to the IFE between deprotonated PVP-AZ NPs and dispersed RQDs. Therefore, the prepared PA-R film can realize colorimetric/fluorescent dual-mode monitoring of food freshness. This study proposes a new strategy for the design of dual-mode smart labels that enable real-time, visual, and in-situ monitoring of food freshness.

AZ (CAS: 72–48-0, molecular formula: C14H8O4, and M.W. 240.21), PVP (CAS: 9003-39-8, molecular formula: (C6H9NO)n, and M.W. ~ 40,000), 4-dimethylaminopyridine (DMAP), melamine (MA), 2,2′-dithiobenzoic acid (DTSA), and PVA (CAS: 9002-89-5, alcoholysis: 98.0–99.0 mol%, viscosity: 5.2–6.0 mPa.s) were supplied by Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Cassava starch (CAS: S25805, solubility: 10 mg/mL, 1 M NaOH) was purchased from Yuanye Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), acetic acid, and triethylamine (TEA) were provided by Macklin Bio-Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Glycerin, ammonia (mass fraction: 25 %), MgCl2, Mg(NO3)2, NaCl, and BaCl2 were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company (Shanghai, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of PVP-AZ NPs and RQDs
ce:section.ce:para: PVP-AZ NPs were prepared via the one-pot method according to the previous report (Xiao, Liu, Kang, Cui, & Xu, 2021). Under magnetic stirring, PVP (0.9 g), DMAP (0.3 g), TEA (0.6 g), and AZ (30 mg) were successively added to a beaker containing 30 mL of DMSO. After 24 h of stirring away from light, the mixture was dialyzed in DMSO aqueous solution (3500 Da) for 1 day to remove unreacted PVP, AZ and other impurities. The resulting solution was continued to be dialyzed in ultrapure water for 2 days and then lyophilized for collection.

The preparation of RQDs were conducted according to Liu's report (Liu, Jiang, et al., 2022). In brief, MA (100.8 mg) and DTSA (272 mg) were dissolved in 20 mL of acetic acid through sonication. Subsequently, the resulting mixture was transferred into a reactor and heated at 180 °C for 10 h. After cooling to room temperature, the reaction solution was dropped into 2 L of boiling water. The suspension was filtered and washed, followed by vacuum-dried at 60 °C for 6 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of PVP-AZ NPs and RQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The micromorphology was characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Hitachi HT7700, Japan, image resolution: 0.204 nm, and acceleration voltage: 40–120 kV). For TEM analysis, the samples were prepared by dropping a solution of PVP-AZ NPs and RQDs onto a copper mesh and then drying it in air before placing it in a microscope (Kanikireddy et al., 2020). And the particle size distribution was analyzed by Nano Measurer software. Hydrodynamic diameter, polydispersity index (PDI), and zeta potential were determined by dynamic light scattering (DLS) instrument (ZSU3100, Malvern Instruments, UK). The surface functional groups were characterized by flourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, NicoletTMiS™ 50, Thermo Scientific, USA). After 32 scans with air as background, spectra were recorded in the wavenumber range of 4000–800 cm−1 with a resolution of 4 cm−1. Before the analysis, the dried samples were mixed with KBr (1 mg sample/200 mg KBr) and molded into homogeneous discs (You et al., 2022). The crystal structure was analyzed using X-ray diffractometry (XRD, D8 Advance, Bruker, Germany) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, ThermoFisher Scientific, USA). For XRD analysis, the scanning angle was 15° to 75° (2θ) at the scanning speed of 5°/min (Xiao et al., 2021). The thermodynamic stability was assessed using a thermogravimetric analyzer (TGA, DTG-60 A, Shimadzu, Japan). The temperature was from room temperature to 550 °C, and the heating rate was 10 °C/min (Ren et al., 2023). Particles were prepared for XPS analysis by a centrifuge washing process. Stock solution (0.2 mL) was first dispersed in 1.5 mL centrifuge tubes, and then ultracentrifuged at 30000 rpm (49,000 ×g maximum, 38,000 ×g average, and 27,000 ×g minimum) for 90 min. After the first centrifugation, the supernatant was pipetted off and the particles redispersed in 0.2 mL of deionized (DI) water. This redispersion was followed by another round of centrifugation and removal of the supernatant. The particles were again redispersed, and another round of centrifugation followed. After each round of centrifugation, a sample was saved for XPS analysis. In each case the supernatant was poured off and 20 μL of DI water was added to the particles “plug” to assist deposition onto a cleaned Si wafer for XPS analysis (Wang, Engelhard, Baer, & Castner, 2016).

The PA-R film was prepared via solution casting method (Xiao et al., 2021). In detail, 3.4 g of cassava starch and 1.7 g of PVA were incorporated into 100 mL of ultrapure water. The mixture was heated at 100 °C for 1 h. After cooling to 40 °C, PVP-AZ NPs aqueous solution (0.25 mg/mL, 2 mL), RQDs ethanol solution (1 mg/mL, 2 mL), and 1 g of glycerol were subsequently added, and the stirring process was continued for 1 h. The film-forming solution was left standing, defoamed, and then cast into the Petri dishes (25 mL/per dish, 90 mm). After drying for 12 h at 55 °C, the film was obtained and further transferred to an environment with 50 % relative humidity for storage at 25 °C. The as-prepared film was named the PA-R film. Besides, the 1 film was obtained by replacing the RQDs ethanol solution with ethanol solution, and the 2 film was constructed by utilizing ultra-pure water instead of PVP-AZ NPs aqueous solution. As for the Blank film, it was prepared by replacing PVP-AZ NPs aqueous solution with ultra-pure water and RQDs ethanol solution with ethanol, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physical and chemical properties of the PA-R film

ce:section.ce:para: The surface and cross-sectional morphology of the prepared films were characterized using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, S-4800, HITACHI, Japan, secondary electron image resolution: 1.0 nm (15 kV, WD = 4 mm), 1.4 nm (1 kV, WD =1.5 mm, deceleration mode), 2.0 nm (1 kV, WD = 1.5 mm, ordinary mode), magnification: 20 x ~ 800 x, and acceleration voltage: 0.5 kV ~ 30 kV, 0.1 kV/step). Prior to measurements, all films were sputtered with metal spray in a vacuum environment (Yang et al., 2023). The Image J software was used to calculate the porosity of SEM images (cross-section) (Gao et al., 2024). The porosity on the surface of the cassava starch/PVA-based films was determined by fullautomatic specific surface and porosity analyzer (Quantachrome Autosorb IQ, USA). The films were crushed to powder in liquid nitrogen. Then, 40 mg of each powdered film was placed at the bottom of a BET tube, which was then evacuated for 12 h. Thereafter, the tube was inserted into a fullautomatic specific surface and porosity analyzer under following conditions: adsorptive gas N2, bath temperature 50 °C (Gao et al., 2023).

The transmittance was recorded by the Shimadzu UV-2600i spectrophotometer using air as the reference. The color parameters (L*, a*, b*) were examined by a colorimeter (CM-5, Konica Minolta, Japan). Each sample was tested at five randomly selected locations, and the ΔE was calculated from the below equation (Xiao et al., 2021):
(1)
△ E =
L 1 ∗
−
L 0 ∗
2
+
a 1 ∗
−
a 0 ∗
2
+
b 1 ∗
−
b 0 ∗
2
where: L1*, a1 *, b1* represent L, a, b values of the sample, and L0*, a0 *, b0* represent L, a, b values of the calibration plate.

The chemical structure was characterized by ATR-FTIR spectroscopy (Thermo Scientific, USA) with a resolution of 4 cm−1 and an average of 32 scans in the range of 4000–600 cm−1. The crystal structure was measured by XRD diffractometer (SmartLab SE, Rigaku, Japan) and the scanning speed was 5°/min (Liu, Jiang, et al., 2022).

The thermal stability of the films was assessed by DTG-60 A thermogravimetric analyzer. The film sample (5 mg) was heated from 30 °C to 600 °C at a heating rate of 10 °C/min under N2 flow rate of 20 mL/min (Xiao et al., 2021).

The films thickness was measured using a digital micrometer at any 10 locations. The stretcher (XLW Type, Jinan Languang, China) was used to determine the mechanical performance of the films (6 × 2 cm). The initial gap of the fixture was set to 4 cm, and the crossover head speed was 2.5 cm min−1 (Yang et al., 2023). The tensile strength (TS) and elongation at break (EB) of the films were recorded.

The water vapor permeability (WVP) was evaluated on the basis of a previous report (Yang et al., 2023). The film sample was covered and sealed on the top of a test cup (inner diameter 10 mm) containing 5 mL of ultrapure water. It was stored under a standard condition (25 °C, 50 % RH) for 2 d and weighed every 2 h. WVP was calculated according to the following equation:
(2)
WVP =
Δm × L
/
Δt × A × Δp

Where Δm/Δt (g/s) represents the slope of the plot of weight loss over time, L(m) denotes the thickness of the film, A(m2) signifies the surface area covered by the film, and Δp(Pa) indicates the difference in water vapor pressure across the film.

An SDC-200S WCA analyzer (China) was used to measure the static water contact angle (WCA). Briefly, the ultrapure water (10 μL) was dropped onto the surface of the film sample (3 × 2 cm), and the WAC was recorded (Yang et al., 2023).

The water content was determined according to the standard method (Yang et al., 2023). Film samples (3 × 3 cm) were weighed (m1) and subsequently subjected to heating in an oven at 105 °C until the constant weight (m2). The water content was calculated using the following formula:
(3)
Water content
%
= (
m 1

m 2
/

× 100 %

The biodegradability of the films was evaluated according to the previous report (Yang et al., 2023). Garden soil (the soil moisture was 83.3 %) removed from large particles was carefully transferred into a paper cup (8 cm). The film sample (3 × 3 cm) of known weight (m1) was then buried at a depth of 3 cm from the soil surface. The surface of the soil was weekly sprayed with ultrapure water (5 mL). Plastic wrap with small holes was placed over the cup top to maintain humidity and breathability. The cups were then transferred to a constant temperature and humidity chamber (25 °C, 75 % RH). After 7, 14, 21, 28 days of storage, the films were removed from the soil, cleaned, and dried in a 50 °C oven for 4 h. The weight was recorded (m2) and the weight loss rate was calculated according to the following formula:
(4)

ce:section.ce:section-title: Response of the PA-R film to ammonia
ce:section.ce:para: The amine response was evaluated by employing ammonia as a model BAs in accordance with the previously reported method (Liu, Jiang, et al., 2022). The film was placed on a 24-well plate and exposed to ammonia vapor for 15 min to observe the color response. The RQDs (3 mg) were dispersed in 3 mL ammonia solution with a pH range of 7–10. At pH = 7, the concentration of the ammonia solution was 0.015 mg/mL. At pH = 8, the concentration of the ammonia solution was 0.1 mg/mL. At pH = 9, the concentration of the ammonia solution was 0.125 mg/mL, and at pH = 10, it reached a concentration of 0.15 mg/mL. Subsequently, emission spectra and UV–vis spectra (200–800 nm) of RQDs solutions were recorded using PE-LS55 spectrophotometer and Shimadzu UV-2600i spectrophotometer (Becker, Gurian, Schot, & Leijten, 2023). Accordingly, a digital camera recorded photo of the above solution under daylight and UV light. The prepared PA-R film was separately immersed into pH = 3, 5, 7, 9, and 11 solutions for 5 min. After removing the surface water with filter paper, the visible morphology of the films was determined with a digital camera.

To improve the detection sensitivity, the content of PVP-AZ NPs in the PA-R film as well as the exposure time of the film in ammonia atmosphere were optimized. The color change of the film to ammonia at 0–25 mg/mL was recorded under the above optimal condition in sunlight and UV light. Furthermore, the PA-R film was alternately exposed to ammonia and acetic acid atmospheres (2.5 mg/mL) for 7 times to evaluate the repeatability. The effect of humidity on detection performance was also investigated. The PA-R film samples with uniform size were placed in an environment with RH of 33 % (MgCl2 saturated solution), 53 % (Mg (NO3)2 saturated solution), 75 % (NaCl saturated solution), and 90 % (BaCl2 saturated solution) for 24 h, respectively (Yang et al., 2023). The film response to 2.5 mg/mL ammonia was investigated. The effect of cassava starch/PVA-based film with different thickness on the detection performance was studied. The film-forming solution (15 mL, 25 mL, 35 mL, and 45 mL) was poured into a disposable petri dish. After drying at 55 °C for 12 h, the film with different thickness was obtained. Then the response of the film to 2.5 mg/mL ammonia was recorded. The stability test of the prepared PA-R film at different storage temperature (25 °C, 4 °C, and − 20 °C) for 4 weeks. The film response to 2.5 mg/mL ammonia was investigated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical application in shrimp freshness monitoring
ce:section.ce:para: Uniformly sized fresh shrimp were placed in sterile petri dishes. And the PA-R film with a size of 1 × 3 cm on the lid of the petri dishes was used as an indicator label for shrimp freshness. It was important to note that the shrimp did not touch the label. The sealed dishes were divided into three groups (nine parallel each). Afterwords, they were kept at 4 °C, 25 °C and − 20 °C for five days, during which the label color was recorded by a camera. The same procedure was performed with wet wipes instead of shrimp samples to eliminate humidity interference. Uniformly sized fresh razor clam was placed in sterile petri dishes. The PA-R film with a size of 1 × 3 cm on the lid of the petri dishes was used as an indicator label for razor clam freshness. The PA-R labels were used to monitor the freshness of razor clams at 25 °C in accordance with the above procedure. The determination of total volatile basic nitrogen (TVB-N) content in shrimp followed the Chinese standard (GB 5009.228–2016). The shrimp sample was homogenized in ten times its volume of water, allowed to stand for 30 min, and then treated with 5 mL (10 g/L) of MgO before distillation. The level of TVB-N was quantified by titrating the distillate with aqueous hydrochloric acid (0.001 M) under the introduction of Kjeldahl nitrogen analyzer (Hanon Instrument, Jinan, China) (Wang et al., 2022).

All experiments were performed more than three times. The data was displayed as mean ± standard deviations (SD) with a significance level of 5 %. Data analysis was conducted using Statistix 8.0 and Origin 2022 software.

PVP-AZ NPs was synthesized in DMSO by coupling method in order to improve the water dispersion of AZ (Fig. 2A) (Manju & Sreenivasan, 2011). It can be seen from the structural formula in Fig. 2B that AZ and PVP were connected by ester bond. The micromorphology of PVP-AZ NPs was analyzed using TEM images. As presented in Fig. 2C, AZ particles had different sizes and exhibited obvious aggregation. The particle size ranged from 300 nm to 2500 nm, with an average size of 1211.2 ± 512.7 nm (n = 195, Fig. 2F). Fig. 2D exhibits that PVP-AZ NPs have a uniform spherical structure with an average particle size of 572.7 ± 145.8 nm (n = 55, Fig. 2G). The decrease of particle size indicated that PVP-AZ NPs displayed better dispersion in the water phase. As shown in Fig. S1, it was observed that the AZ solution presented a form of aggregation, which might be due to the poor water dispersion of AZ. Compared with pure AZ, the prepared PVP-AZ NPs solution was clear without any visible precipitation. Hydrophilic PVP can be used as a modifier to form nanoparticles through coupling, which greatly improved the water solubility of AZ (Liu et al., 2017). DLS data in Fig. 2E also confirmed the size change. Furthermore, the decrease in PDI value from 1 to 0.4 also provided strong support for superior water dispersion of PVP-AZ NPs. Noticeably, the particle size difference between the two measurements was due to the fact that the hydrodynamic diameter included an extended molecular layer of the material while the particles in the TEM image were in a dry state (Wilson & Prud'Homme, 2021). Fig. 2H displays the Zeta potentials of AZ and PVP-AZ NPs are −32 mV and − 8 mV, respectively. The increase in potential may be caused by the positive charge generated by the protonation of CN in PVP (Manju & Sreenivasan, 2011).

The chemical structure of the prepared PVP-AZ NPs was analyzed using FTIR spectroscopy. As displayed in Fig. 2I, the characteristic peaks at 3428 cm−1 and 1450 cm−1 were caused by the tensile vibrations of OH and NH of AZ and PVP, respectively. The OH and NH characteristic peaks of PVP-AZ NPs were shifted compared with those of AZ and PVP, which may be due to the formation of intermolecular hydrogen bonds between AZ and PVP (Manju & Sreenivasan, 2011). The peak with 1649 cm−1 as the center was attributed to the destruction of the ring structure and the formation of ester bond during esterification. The crystal structure was characterized by XRD pattern. As displayed in Fig. 2J, AZ exhibited a distinct sharp and narrow diffraction peak at 2θ = 26°, reflecting its semi-crystalline structure. In comparison, PVP-AZ NPs showed a weak and wide diffraction peak at 2θ = 22°, indicating the amorphous structure of PVP-AZ NPs (Najafabadi et al., 2023). Further, the thermal stability of PVP-AZ NPs was investigated. As visualized in Fig. 2K, the thermal decomposition behavior of PVP-AZ NPs was mainly divided into three stages. The first weight loss occurred at 34.4–114.6 °C with a weight loss of 4.2 %, which was caused by the evaporation of water in the sample (Yang et al., 2023). The second weight loss occurring at 114.6–338.2 °C was due to the volatilization of other loose residual mixtures present in the sample (Xiao et al., 2021). The most obvious weight loss (83.5 %) occurred in the range of 338.2–563.9 °C, which might be attributed to the degradation of side chain groups of PVP (Wu et al., 2023). The inset in Fig. 2K presents the change in the rate of thermal decomposition. It can be seen that the maximum thermal degradation temperature of PVP-AZ NPs (437 °C) was higher than that of AZ (281.6 °C), reflecting that the coupling of PVP and AZ significantly enhanced the stability of AZ.

RQDs were prepared by solvothermal method with DTSA and MA as precursors (Liu, Jiang, et al., 2022). The powder appeared bright red in daylight (Fig. 3A), and the microstructure was characterized by HR-TEM. As shown in Fig. S2, the hydrodynamic diameters of RQDs in ethanol solution and aqueous solution were 2.3 nm and 52.9 nm, respectively. The increase of particle size indicated that RQDs formed aggregates in water. HR-TEM images revealed that RQDs dispersed in ethanol showed a uniformly dispersed spherical structure (Fig. 3B), while RQDs dispersed in water displayed a clustered cross-linked state (Fig. S3A). The corresponding mean particle size varied from 2.0 ± 0.4 nm (n = 100) (Fig. 3B) to 48.6 ± 8.9 nm (n = 18) (Fig. S3B). This further confirmed that RQDs were hydrophobic and could form aggregates in aqueous solution. Fig. S4 presented that the zeta potentials of the RQDs in ethanol solution and aqueous solution were 51.70 mV and 6.93 mV, respectively. The high potential indicated that RQDs had excellent dispersibility and stability in ethanol solution, but tended to aggregate in aqueous solution. Therefore, the above data confirmed the hydrophobicity and AIE effect of RQDs.

The chemical structure of the prepared RQDs was characterized using FT-IR and XPS spectra. The FTIR spectra were shown in Fig. 3C. The peak of hydrophilic OH stretching vibration from MA at 3464 cm−1 and the NH vibration peak belonging to DTSA at 3131 cm−1 were not observed in the spectrum of RQDs. The disappearance of these hydrophilic functional groups may be caused by the amidation and carbonization reactions of MA and DTSA (Yang et al., 2019). In addition, RQDs presented characteristic peaks of hydrophobic groups such as SH, amide‑carbonyl, benzene ring, and aromatic groups at 2657 cm−1, 1678 cm−1, 1456 cm−1, and 1251 cm−1. The hydrophilic hydroxyl group (3494 cm−1) derived from DTSA after amidation and carbonization did not completely disappear on the surface of RQDs but existed in a small amount. These data indicated that the prepared RQDs had hydrophobicity. The XPS spectrum in Fig. S5A showed that RQDs presented four peaks at 283.98 eV, 531.58 eV, 398.58 eV, and 163.08 eV, which indicated that RQDs were composed of C (69.25 %), O (19.88 %), S (9.18 %), and N (1.69 %) elements. The high-resolution C 1 s spectrum shown in Fig. S5B was deconvolved into peaks corresponding to the C-C/C=C (283.98 eV) and C=O/C=N (287.58 eV) functional groups, respectively (Boonta et al., 2020). In Fig. S5C, the S2P spectrum presented three peaks, which appeared at 163.08 eV, 163.50 eV, and 164.01 eV, corresponding to SH, SC, and SS bonds, respectively (Yang et al., 2019). The N 1 s band exhibited two peaks at 398.68 eV and 398.58 eV, respectively, which correspond to pyridinic C3 N and pyrrolic C2-N-H groups (Fig. S5D) (Yang et al., 2019). The above data together showed that there were almost no amino on the surface of RQDs, which was consistent with the FTIR results.

The UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence emission spectra of RQDs dissolved in ethanol-water solutions were analyzed for the sake of further reveal their optical properties. Fig. 3D demonstrates that the RQDs solutions exhibit distinct absorption peaks at 310 nm, which are caused by the π-π* transition of CC within the RQDs core. Besides, the peak intensity exhibited a gradual decrease as the water content increased. Correspondingly, the RQDs solutions changed from clear and transparent to cloudy under daylight (Fig. 3E), which may be due to the hydrophobicity of RQDs leading to particle aggregation. Notably, under UV light, the clarified RQDs solutions with low water content emitted blue fluorescence, while the turbid RQDs solutions with high water content emitted red fluorescence. The fluorescence emission spectra in Fig. 3F also verified this fluorescence color transformation. The intensity of the blue fluorescence peak at 443 nm gradually decreased with the increase of water content. When the water content exceeded 50 %, the fluorescence peak at 443 nm almost disappeared, while a new red fluorescence peak appeared at 603 nm. This phenomenon was attributed to the aggregation of RQDs though π − π stacking, which restricted the intramolecular rotation of RQDs, thereby activating the AIE process (Yang et al., 2019). This unique optical property was expected to be applied to fluorescence analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physical and chemical properties of the PA-R film

ce:section.ce:para: SEM images showed the microstructure of cassava starch/PVA-based films prepared by casting method. As shown in Fig. 4A-D, the surfaces of the four films were smooth, dense, and flat without obvious defects. However, many irregular voids were observed on the cross-section of the films (Fig. 4E-H), which may be related to the evaporation rate of solvents on the film surface and inside (Fu et al., 2023; Gaur, Mishra, Gautam, Singh, & Yasir, 2014; Pervin, Ghosh, & Basavaraj, 2019). Specifically, the surface layer of the film-forming solution was in full contact with the hot air, so that the water molecules in the surface layer were heated to evaporate. Water evaporation drove dispersed cassava starch/PVA polymer particles to form tightly packed ordered arrays. With the continuous evaporation, a complete dense film was gradually formed on the surface of the film-forming solution, which hindered the rapid evaporation of water in the underlying solution and prolonged the film-forming time. However, due to the difference in the weight and size of the matrix particles, it was difficult for the matrix particles to form a tightly ordered array under the combined action of gravity and water evaporation force. This resulted in the appearance of irregular voids in the interior of the films. By controlling the drying rate and temperature, it can affect the size and distribution of voids (Samanta & Bordes, 2020). The void size of the 1 film did not change significantly compared with that of Blank film, while the void sizes of the 2 film and the PA-R film were significantly smaller. It meant that the effect of adding a small amount of PVP-AZ NPs on the film structure was negligible. On the contrary, the addition of RQDs was relatively large, which made the film structure denser. When the ethanol solution evaporated rapidly, RQDs accumulated in the film matrix and filled these voids, making the film denser.

Further, the porosity of cassava starch/PVA-based film was analyzed by nitrogen adsorption-analysis isothermal curve (Fig. S6). The specific surface area of the Blank film was determined by Bruner-Emmett-Teller (BET) to be 0.082 cm3/g (Fig. S6A). After addition of PVP-AZ nanoparticles (NPs), RQDs, and PVP-AZ NPs + RQDs, the BET specific surface areas of the films were 0.033 cm3/g, 0.519 cm3/g, and 0.529 cm3/g, respectively (Fig. S6C, E, and G). BET specific surface area increased when RQDs were mixed, indicating that the specific surface area was mainly affected by RQDs. When RQDs were added to the film matrix, it may be due to the rapid evaporation of ethanol during the drying process. And the accumulation of RQDs in the film-forming solution from the dispersion state to the aggregation state could increase its specific surface area. In addition, non-local density function theory (NLDFT) results showed that all samples had a pore volume of 0, which meant the denser structure of the prepared films (Fig. S6B, D, F, and H). The results were also confirmed by SEM, indicating that the pores on the cross-section of the film did not penetrate the surface of the film.

The films' appearance was depicted in Fig. 4I-L. The Blank film was colorless and transparent, and the color of the film after the introduction of PVP-AZ NPs alone presented a light yellow color derived from the color of PVP-AZ NPs themself. When red RQDs was added to the film matrix, the 2 film showed obvious red color. Orange yellow PA-R film was obtained by adding red RQDs and yellow PVP-AZ NPs to the film matrix. The color parameters of all films were exhibited in Table 1 . The presence of PVP-AZ NPs resulted in a decrease in redness (a*) and an increase in yellowness (b*) (p < 0.05). However, the addition of RQDs led to an obvious decrease in the film brightness (L* value) and an increase in the films' redness (a*) and yellowness (b*) (p < 0.05). Notably, there was no distinct difference in the a* and L* values between the 2 film and the PA-R film, which corresponded to the macroscopic photograph. The light barrier properties of the prepared films were further investigated. As displayed in Fig. 4M, the Blank film had a high light transmittance. The incorporation of PVP-AZ NPs and RQDs reduced the light transmittance, which was caused by light absorption by PVP-AZ NPs and RQDs (Fig. 4N). It was worth noting that the light transmission of the 2 film was lower than that of the PVP-AZ NPs film, which meant the fact that the filling effect of RQDs made the network structure of the film denser, thereby reducing the light transmission. Compared to other films, the PA-R film had the lowest transmittance, reflecting its excellent optical barrier performance.

As shown in Fig. 4O, the characteristic peaks of the Blank film at 3342 cm−1, 2943 cm−1 and 1653 cm−1 were caused by the stretching vibrations of OH and CH in cassava starch and OH in bound water. The characteristic peak at 1413 cm−1 was originated from the bending vibration of CH-CH2 in PVA (Qin et al., 2020). The ATR-FTIR spectra of the four films were similar, which possibly due to the low addition of PVP-AZ NPs and RQDs, or the absence of strong chemical reactions between cassava starch/PVA matrix and PVP-AZ NPs and RQDs. According to the previous report, physical entanglement and non-covalent interactions (such as hydrogen bonds, van der Waals forces, etc.) were considered to be the main driving forces between the connecting film components (Xiao et al., 2021). The relative absorbance ratio of A4000–2995/A993 was used to measure the hydrogen bond strength (Yu et al., 2018). As shown in Fig. S7, the hydrogen bond strength of A3335/A993 films increased significantly with the addition of PVP-AZ NPs and RQDs. These results showed that after the addition of PVP-AZ NPs and RQDs, more hydrogen bond interactions were formed between them and cassava starch/PVA matrix. The crystal structure of the films was characterized by XRD patterns (Fig. 4P). No characteristic peak of cassava starch was observed at 2θ = 10.7° and 2θ = 25.6°, probably owing to the breakdown of the semi-crystalline structure of starch particles during gelatinization (Tavares, Campos, Mitsuyuki, Luchesi, & Marconcini, 2019). Two diffraction peaks appeared at 2θ = 12.8° and 2θ = 19.6° in all film samples, which were related to the semi-crystalline structure of PVA (Qin et al., 2020). Interestingly, the addition of PVP-AZ NPs and RQDs significantly reduced the intensity of diffraction peak at 2θ = 12.8°. On the one hand, the hydrogen bond between PVP-AZ NPs and PVA destroyed the interaction between matrix molecules (Li, Wang, Feng, Zhuang, & Zhu, 2024). On the other hand, the formation of hydrogen bonds between the N atom of amide carbonyl group of RQDs and oxygen-rich functional group changed the network structure of crosslinked by PVA (Nazim & Kim, 2020).

Fig. 5A displays the thicknesses of the cassava starch/PVA-based films. The addition of PVP-AZ NPs and RQDs had no statistical significance on the thickness of the films (p > 0.05), which meant the small addition of PVP-AZ NPs and RQDs and their uniform dispersion in the matrix.

Mechanical properties including EB and TS of the films were measured. As interpreted in Fig. 5B and C, the EB ranged from 12.11 % to 16.03 %, indicating the excellent ductility of the prepared films. The TS of the films in the range of 31.2–38.9 MPa reflected their outstanding flexibility (Ma, Liang, Cao, & Wang, 2018). However, there was no significant difference in EB (p > 0.05) and TS (p > 0.05) among the films, suggesting the excellent compatibility of PVP-AZ NPs, RQDs, and the film matrix.

The water barrier properties of the films were assessed by measuring WVP and water content. As depicted schematically in Fig. 5D, the film's WVP ranged from 2.10 × 10−10–2.67 × 10−10 g Pa−1 s−1. The difference between them was not statistically significant, demonstrating that the water vapor blocking ability of the film was not changed by the addition of PVP-AZ NPs and RQDs. However, the WVP of the cassava starch/PVA-based films was higher than that of pectin film and Pullulan composite film (Jiang, Zhang, Cao, & Jiang, 2022; Kang et al., 2023), which displayed facilitate the entry of TVB-N and water vapor into the film to cause color reactions.

The addition of PVP-AZ NPs and RQDs had no significant effect on EB, TS, and WVP of the films (p > 0.05). Further, the SEM images of the cross-section were analyzed using Image J software (Fig. S8), and their porosity were calculated (Fig. S9). It can be seen that there was no statistical difference in cross section porosity of the four films (p > 0.05). This indicated that the internal densification degree of cassava starch/PVA-based films was similar.

The water content of the films ranged from 2.45 % to 2.56 % (Fig. 5E), which was lower than that of the reported soy protein isolate film and corn amylose film (Xiao et al., 2021; Yang et al., 2023). It was possibly due to the strong hydrogen bond interaction between cassava starch and PVA, which prevented the binding of matrix molecules to water molecules (Chen, Zong, Wang, & Xie, 2021). The hydrophilicity was reflected by WCA (Fig. 5F). The WCA of all films was in the range of 60.2° ~ 62.7°, which reflected good hydrophilicity. It was beneficial for TVB-N to adhere to the film surface along with water molecules. However, no statistical difference existed between WCA, indicating that the incorporation of PVP-AZ NPs and RQDs did not alter the hydrophilicity of the cassava starch/PVA-based films.

According to the TGA curves, the thermal degradation process was divided into three weightlessness stages (Fig. 5G). The initial weight loss occurring in the range of 30 °C to 221 °C with a weight loss of 6.4 %. It was mainly related to the water evaporation and the glycerol decomposition (Yang et al., 2023). The weight loss in the second stage occurred at 221.0–405.8 °C with a weight loss of 71.5 %, which might be associated to the thermal decomposition of cassava starch and PVA (Chen et al., 2021). The third stage of weightlessness occurred between 405.8 °C and 600.0 °C with a weight loss of 11.5 %, which was caused by the carbonization of the film matrix (Almeida et al., 2023). The DTG curves in the illustration reflected the thermal decomposition rate. The Blank film displayed the maximum thermal decomposition rate of 331.9 °C. After addition of PVP-AZ NPs, RQDs and PVP-AZ NPs/RQDs, the maximum thermal decomposition rate appeared at 332.7 °C, 334.8 °C and 334.1 °C, respectively. The results showed that the addition of PVP-AZ NPs and RQDs could improve the thermal stability of the film, it was attributed to the hydrogen bond interaction between the additive and the film-forming matrix (Lin et al., 2021).

The biodegradability of the films was analyzed by recording the changes of macromorphology and weight during burial in soil (Fig. 5H and I). After 7 days of burial, the film surface became rough, while the structure remained intact, and the weight loss reached 17.9 %. Most of the films were decomposed after 28 days of storage, with a weight loss above 70 % (p > 0.05). The above data reflected that the prepared films had good biodegradability. No difference in weight loss indicated that the degradation of the films was primarily dependent on the film-forming matrix. As a natural polymer, cassava starch/PVA-based network structure was easily permeated by soil water, resulting in the polymer chain being weakened and decomposed by soil microorganisms (Hasan et al., 2020).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Possible response mechanism of the PA-R film to ammonia
ce:section.ce:para: The response behavior of the films to ammonia was investigated. Fig. 6A illustrates that neither the Blank film nor the 2 film showed obvious color changes before and after reaction with ammonia in daylight. However, after reacting with ammonia, the color of the 1 film changed from light yellow to purple, and the color of the PA-R film changed from orange yellow to purple. It indicated that the film's color response to ammonia under daylight was closely related to the PVP-AZ NPs. Under UV irradiation, neither the Blank film nor the 1 film emitted fluorescent signal before and after reaction with ammonia. However, the fluorescence of the 2 film after reaction with ammonia varied from red to blue, which revealed the possibility of RQDs as a fluorescent indicator for ammonia. The PA-R film also exhibited a similar fluorescent color change. Specially, the blue fluorescence intensity of the PA-R film was distinctly lower than that of the 2 film. It may be due to the conformational change of PVP-AZ NPs, which caused the quenching of RQDs fluorescence through IFE. The above results indicated that the prepared PA-R film exhibited obvious color change to ammonia under both daylight and UV light, and it was expected to be an ideal candidate for dual-channel smart labels to detect BAs.

In order to verify the mechanism of the PA-R film response to ammonia, the UV–vis absorption spectra and photos of the PVP-AZ NPs solutions at various pH was recorded. As depicted in Fig. 6B, the PVP-AZ NPs solution exhibited the maximum absorption peak at 425 nm at pH = 2, and this absorption peak disappeared with the increase of pH. A new peak appeared at 537 nm when pH = 7, and the solution color transitioned from light yellow to light purple. It was mainly caused by the deprotonation of the phenol hydroxyl group of PVP-AZ NPs (Fig. 6C) (Ezati et al., 2020). When the pH continued to increase, the intensity of the absorption peak exhibited an upward trend and the solution color transitioned from light purple to dark purple. It indicated that PVP-AZ NPs can serves as a response signal to ammonia in daylight. As depicted in Fig. S10, the color of the film underwent a transition from orange yellow to purple in daylight as the pH value increased from 3 to 11. Correspondingly, under UV light, the color of the film transitioned successively from red to blue. The color change of the film under alkaline conditions indicated that the film was responsive to pH, which corresponded to the color change of the film after fumigation with ammonia.

The fluorescence emission spectra of the RQDs solutions under alkaline condition were recorded at 365 nm excitation wavelength. As depicted in Fig. 6D, with the increase of pH, the fluorescence peak intensity at 603 nm decreased, while the intensity of the peak at 443 nm gradually increased. Accordingly, the solutions changed from cloudy to clear, and the fluorescence varied from red to blue. This may be ascribed to the amplification of the distorted intramolecular charge transfer effect caused by the dispersion of RQDs in highly polar solutions, thus preventing π-π accumulation and preventing the formation of the AIE effect (Fig. 6G) (Kushwaha, Kumar, Kumar, & Singh, 2022). Furthermore, the fluorescence spectra of the RQDs solution and the UV–vis absorption spectrum of the PVP-AZ NPs solution were recorded under alkaline condition. As illustrated in Fig. 6E, the RQDs solution showed an obvious fluorescence emission peak at 443 nm at 365 nm excitation wavelength. The PVP-AZ NPs solution displayed the maximum absorption peak at 537 nm due to deprotonation. The fluorescence emission spectrum displayed partial overlap with the absorption spectrum, and the emission peak intensity decreased with the increase of the PVP-AZ NPs concentration (Fig. 6F). This demonstrated that the deprotonated PVP-AZ NPs quenched the fluorescence of RQDs through IFE. It was inferred that the fluorescence attenuation of the PA-R film after reaction with ammonia was caused by IFE compared with that of the 2 film. The above results showed that the prepared PA-R film can achieve color response to ammonia through the deprotonation of PVP-AZ NPs under daylight, and fluorescence response by RQDs dispersion and IFE under UV light.

Key experimental parameters including the addition amount of PVP-AZ NPs and the ammonia fumigation time, were optimized to improve the detection performance of the films. As can be seen from Fig. 7A, the color change under sunlight of the PA-R film before and after reaction with ammonia tended to be more apparent with the increase of PVP-AZ NPs concentration. After the concentration reached 0.25 mg/mL, the color change of the film was easy to be recognized by the naked eye. Under UV light, the blue fluorescence of the film after reacting with ammonia gradually decreased with the increase of the concentration of PVP-AZ NPs, and the fluorescence intensity did not decrease when the concentration of PVP-AZ NPs exceeded 0.25 mg/mL. This indicated that the deprotonated PVP-AZ NPs achieved maximum fluorescence quenching of RQDs through IFE. Therefore, 0.25 mg/mL PVP-AZ NPs was selected as the optimal concentration for the preparation of PA-R film. The effect of reaction time on the ammonia response behavior of the PA-R film was further assessed. As presented in Fig. 7B, the PA-R film’ color in daylight transitioned from orange yellow to dark yellow and then to purple with the extension of reaction time. Correspondingly, the fluorescence of the film turned from red to blue under UV light, and the blue fluorescence was gradually quenched. After the reaction time exceeded 16 min, the color changes in the film tended to be stable under daylight and UV light. Therefore, 16 min was selected as the optimal ammonia response time for the PA-R film.

Under the above optimal reaction conditions, the sensitivity of the prepared PA-R film to ammonia was further analyzed. Fig. 7C displays that the PA-R film underwent a color transformation under daylight from orange yellow to dark yellow and then to purple with the increase of ammonia concentration. Under UV light, the fluorescence of the film turned from red to light red and then to faint blue. When the ammonia concentration reached 2.5 mg/mL, the color response of the film to ammonia remained stable. Therefore, 2.5 mg/mL of ammonia can be selected as a simulation agent for the BAs response of the film. And an ammonia concentration of 2.5 mg/mL was chosen as the ammonia concentration for the test. The observer can easily discern this distinctive double-channel color transformation with naked eye, indicating that the PA-R film can realize visual and sensitive detection of ammonia. In addition, the film's reusability was depicted in Fig. S11. After reacting with ammonia, the film was placed in acetic acid atmosphere for 16 min. The film color returned to its pre-reaction state under daylight and UV light. After alternating exposure to ammonia vapor and acetic acid vapor for 6 times, the film still maintained a sensitive colorimetric and fluorescent response to ammonia. This data demonstrated that the prepared PA-R film had excellent reusability. The reusability of the film is higher than that reported by Yang et al. (2023). It was mainly due to PVP forming AZ nanoparticles through coupling, so that AZ was protected from extreme environment damage. With the increase of thickness, the color change of the PA-R film before and after ammonia reaction remained consistent, indicating that the thickness of thick cassava starch/PVA-based film did not affect the label's dual-channel monitoring of BAs (Fig. S12).

Ambient humidity is also an important factor affecting the color responsiveness of the film. As depicted in Fig. 7D, the colorimetric and fluorescent response of the film to ammonia was more obvious in higher humidity environment. It may be due to the fact that higher ambient humidity facilitated ammonia adhere to the film surface along with water molecules and caused color changes (Yang et al., 2023). Therefore, the developed PA-R film was suitable for the colorimetric/fluorescent dual channel monitoring of ammonia in high humidity environment. Photoshop software was used to read the color changes of the films. The magenta (M) and cyan (C) values in the Photoshop CMYK values were extracted from the color of the film under daylight and UV light, respectively. As exhibited in Fig. S13, the color of PA-R film did not change significantly during 4 weeks of storage under daylight and 365 nm UV light under three storage temperature conditions. The color change of the film before and after the reaction with ammonia were basically stable. The corresponding M, C, ΔM, and ΔC showed no significant change, which also verified this result. It can be concluded that the prepared PA-R film can maintain its color stability and sensitive response to BAs within 4 weeks of storage at different temperatures (25 °C, 4 °C, and − 20 °C). It was mainly due to PVP forming AZ nanoparticles through coupling, so that the structure of AZ itself was protected from the damage of extreme environment. However, the storage stability of PA-R film over longer periods should be investigated in the future.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the PA-R film in freshness monitoring
ce:section.ce:para: The practical application potential of the PA-R film was evaluated using it as an indicator label for shrimp freshness monitoring. Accordingly, Photoshop software was used to read the color changes of the labels. The M and C values in the Photoshop CMYK values were extracted from the color of the film under daylight and UV light, respectively. The PA-R label was pasted on top of and sealed inside a plastic container containing shrimp. The container was kept at three different temperatures (25 °C, 4 °C, and − 20 °C) for 5 days, and the dynamic changes in shrimp freshness over time were monitored. As shown in Fig. S14, the color of the label did not change significantly within 4 h. When the storage time transitioned from 7 h to 18 h, the color of the label changed from orange yellow (M value range in daylight: 46 ± 3) to purple (M value range in daylight: 55 ± 2) in daylight. Correspondingly, the fluorescence color of the label under UV light changed from red (C value range in UV light: 68 ± 3) to blue (C value range in UV light: 77 ± 2), indicating that the shrimp had been decomposed to produce BAs. After 1 day of storage, the color of the label was purple under daylight and blue under UV light. After the shrimp was stored at 4 °C for 3 days (Fig. 8A and C), the indicator label transitioned from orange yellow (M value range in daylight: 38 ± 1) to purple (M value range in daylight: 60 ± 1) under daylight. Correspondingly, the red fluorescence of the label gradually turned to blue (C value range in UV light: 75 ± 1) fluorescence under UV light, indicating that the shrimp was spoiled. However, the color and fluorescence of the label barely changed after the shrimp was stored at −20 °C for 5 days, reflecting that the shrimp was still fresh. Except the shrimp, the prepared PA-R label was also used to monitor the spoilage of razor clam stored at 25 °C within 24 h (Fig. S15). As the storage time extended, the color of the PA-R label changed from orange yellow (0–4 h) (M value range in daylight: 38 ± 1) to dark yellow (7 h) (M value range in daylight: 43 ± 1) to purple (18 h) (M value range in daylight: 54 ± 2) in daylight. Accordingly, the label's color shifted from red (0–4 h) (C value range in UV light: 47 ± 2) to faint red (7 h) (C value range in UV light: 60 ± 3) and finally to blue (18 h) (C value range in UV light: 77 ± 3) under UV light. These observations indicated that the razor clam had deteriorated to produce BAs. After 1 day of storage, the color of the label changed under both daylight and UV light.

The content of TVB-N in shrimp samples was further determined to verify the accuracy of the PA-R label for monitoring shrimp freshness. TVB-N value of shrimp: <12 mg/100 g represents fresh, 12 mg/100 g ~ 20 mg/100 g indicates medium fresh but edible, >20 mg/100 g demonstrates spoiled (Jia et al., 2019). As exhibited in Fig. 8B, after the shrimp was stored at 25 °C for one day, the TVB-N content was 63 ± 17 mg/100 g. The TVB-N content of shrimp stored at 4 °C for three days was 37 ± 5.5 mg/100 g. They both exceeded the TVB-N limit for edible shrimp (20 mg/100 g), inferring that they were spoiled. This data was consistent with the color response of the indicator label. The TVB-N content of shrimp stored at 4 °C for 2 days was 18.9 ± 0.9 mg/100 g, which indicated that the shrimp was medium fresh but edible (Fig. S16). The TVB-N content of the shrimp stored at −20 °C for 5 days was <10 mg/100 g, confirming that it was still fresh. These data demonstrated that the color variations of the PA-R label were consistent with the standard TVB-N method, which verified the accuracy of the film in dynamic monitoring of shrimp freshness. In order to exclude the influence of ambient humidity on the color variations of the label, the label was placed in a petri dish containing wet wipes and its color was recorded under the same experimental conditions. As depicted in Fig. 8D, no significant change in the color and fluorescence of the label were observed within 5 days, reflecting that the above color variations were caused by BAs produced by shrimp spoilage. Therefore, the PA-R film developed in this work can act as an indicator label to realize real-time, sensitive, and visual monitoring for shrimp freshness. In addition, the relationship between the lgCTVB-N concentration of shrimp at 25 °C and the color parameters of the film (M value in daylight, C value in ultraviolet light) was further investigated. As displayed in Fig. S17A, the lgCTVB-N displayed a significant linear relationship with M value in the range of lg5 mg/100 g to lg32 mg/100 g. The regression equation was expressed as y = 31.051 lgx + 11.191 (R2 = 0.998). Corresponding, the C value was linearly correlated with lgCTVB-N concentration within the range of lg5 mg/100 g to lg32 mg/100 g, and the linear regression equation in Fig. S17B was y = 41.504 lgx + 19.995 (R2 = 0.992). When the content of lgC TVB-N was <1 g12 mg/100 g, the M value under sunlight was <45, and the C value under fluorescence was <65, indicating that the shrimp was in a fresh state. When the content of lgCTVB-N was 1 g12-1 g20 mg/100 g, the M-value range was between 45 and 52 under daylight, and the C value range was between 65 and 74, which displayed that the freshness of shrimp was medium fresh but edible. When the content of lgCTVB-N was >1 g20 mg/100 g, the M value under daylight was >52, and the corresponding label color under UV light was >74, which demonstrated that the freshness of the shrimp had deteriorated and was not edible.

In this work, a novel, biodegradable, BAs-responsive PA-R film was developed by co-embedding the synthesized PVP-AZ NPs with excellent water dispersion and RQDs with AIE effect into cassava starch/PVA matrix. In the presence of BAs, PVP-AZ NPs underwent a conformational transformation due to deprotonation, causing the PA-R film changing from orange yellow to purple in daylight. Under UV light, RQDs changed from red fluorescence to blue fluorescence by solvent dispersion. Further, the deprotonated PVP-AZ NPs quenched the blue fluorescence of the PA-R film through the IFE. It was used as an intelligent indicator label to monitor shrimp freshness, and the dual-channel color responses were consistent with the detection results of TVB-N method. This study provides a new strategy for the design of colorimetric/fluorescent dual-channel labels, and the constructed smart label has important public health significance and economic value in the field of food safety.

Yingran Xu: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Data Curation. Kai Wang: Methodology. Pan Liu: Resources. Zheng Wang: Resources. Wenya Liu: Visualization, Software. Zan Yang: Visualization. Wanqi Jiang: Visualization. Yibin Zhou: Formal analysis. Mingming Zheng: Software. Yaqing Xiao: Validation. Yingnan Liu: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.
